title: Dallas Police Revises ICE Cooperation Policy Under Gov. Abbott's $90M FIFA Funding Threat
detail: Dallas Police Chief Daniel Comeaux announced on April 23–24, 2026 that the department would revise its immigration enforcement policy (General Order 315.04) after Texas Governor Greg Abbott threatened to pull nearly $90 million in state funding — including more than $55 million in FIFA World Cup security grants (the tournament less than two months away) and over $32 million in public safety grants. The revised policy removed a provision that barred officers from prolonging a person's detention to hold them for ICE agents, and now allows officers to ask detained or arrested individuals about their immigration status. City Manager Kimberly Tolbert wrote a letter to Abbott stating the revisions 'further clarify DPD's continued compliance with state law regarding immigration enforcement.' Key boundaries retained in the revised policy: officers may still not stop or contact people solely to determine immigration status; officers may not ask victims, witnesses, or people reporting crimes about their immigration status unless necessary for an investigation. Texas Rep. Venton Jones (D-Dallas) defended the original policy as protecting 'constitutional rights' and stated it did not prohibit federal cooperation. Chief Comeaux emphasized: 'Our mission has not changed and DPD exists to protect the safety of everyone in Dallas, and we will not stop individuals only to determine their immigration status.' Abbott's directive contended DPD's prior order violated Texas Senate Bill 4 and SB 11, laws requiring local agencies to cooperate with ICE on criminal immigration holds. The Dallas capitulation represents a significant victory for Abbott's campaign to end Texas sanctuary city-style policies and sets a precedent for other major Texas municipalities facing similar federal and state funding pressure.

media.caption: Dallas Police Chief Comeaux revised ICE cooperation policy after Gov. Abbott threatened $90M in state and FIFA World Cup funding

title: National Day of Action Against ICE Warehouse Detention Announced for April 25: 150+ Events in 33 States
detail: On April 24, 2026, Axios reported on an imminent national day of action organized by the Detention Watch Network, Disappeared in America, Indivisible, and allied organizations, scheduled for April 25. Over 150 events were confirmed across at least 33 states, focused on opposing the Trump administration's plan to convert industrial warehouses into mass immigration detention centers. The DHS warehouse expansion plan targets a 100,000+ bed detention capacity — up from the current ~60,000 — by acquiring or converting approximately 23 warehouses nationwide, each designed to hold between 1,500 and 10,000 people. The $38 billion detention expansion initiative, first reported by Bloomberg in January 2026, has faced community resistance: activists had already blocked 13 proposed warehouse conversions across the country by April 2026. The April 25 national day of action was a direct response to what advocates call a growing 'gulag network.' Protests were planned in Rochester, NY; Madison, WI; Rockland, ME; Spokane, WA; Flagstaff, AZ; Mesa, AZ; Surprise, AZ; and dozens of other cities. Key demands from organizers: visible, public opposition to ICE detention expansion before DHS can 'quietly expand detention capacity without public awareness'; elected officials to use every tool available to disrupt conversion permits and deny public funding enabling detention expansion.

media.caption: April 25, 2026 National Day of Action promotional graphic — 'Communities Not Cages: Stop ICE Warehouse Detention'

title: Federal Appeals Court Rules Trump's Executive Order Suspending Asylum at Southern Border Violates U.S. Law
detail: A federal appeals court on April 24, 2026 ruled that President Trump's executive order suspending the right to seek asylum at the southern border is illegal under the Immigration and Nationality Act (INA) — one of the most significant judicial limits on presidential immigration power in years. The court found that Congress, through the INA, explicitly granted individuals the right to apply for asylum regardless of how they entered the country, and that the president cannot override that statutory right through executive order or 'national emergency' and 'invasion' declarations. The panel held that the INA does not authorize the president to remove asylum applicants under 'procedures of his own making' or to suspend their statutory right to apply. The ruling ordered the border reopened to asylum seekers who had been turned back under the executive order. The Trump administration invoked the same legal theory it had used in the Alien Enemies Act cases — that a flow of migrants constitutes an 'invasion' empowering the president to take extraordinary immigration actions — but the court rejected this framing as applied to asylum rights established by statute. The administration announced it would appeal the ruling, with legal observers expecting the case to reach the Supreme Court. Al Jazeera and international outlets noted the ruling sets up one of the most consequential immigration cases in decades. At the border, CBP began processing asylum seekers who had been waiting while the executive order was in effect.

media.caption: Federal appeals court rules Trump's executive order suspending asylum at the southern border violates the Immigration and Nationality Act — a ruling expected to reach the Supreme Court
